edges.description: Reduced circulating fibrinogen limits the substrate available for thrombin-mediated conversion to fibrin.
Reduced hepatic fibrinogen output produces the defining low circulating fibrinogen concentration.
Impaired fibrin clot formation predisposes to a level-dependent bleeding tendency.
A level-dependent bleeding diathesis manifests clinically as variable abnormal bleeding.
In the mild quantitative deficiency state, bleeding is most often provoked by trauma or surgery rather than occurring spontaneously.
Reduced clot stability can manifest as heavy menstrual bleeding in affected women.
Insufficient fibrinogen can compromise placental integrity during pregnancy, producing obstetrical complications.

nodes.description: Bleeding is variable and level-dependent; hypofibrinogenemic patients are frequently asymptomatic, with symptomatic bleeding usually provoked by trauma or surgery.

evidence.snippet: In afibrinogenemia bleeding is the main symptom, whereas in hypofibrinogenemia patients are more frequently asymptomatic.

nodes.description: The bleeding tendency is mild and strongly correlated with the residual coagulant fibrinogen level; many patients are asymptomatic, and bleeding is typically provoked by trauma or surgery rather than spontaneous.

evidence.snippet: a mean fibrinogen activity level of at least 0.7 g/L protected from spontaneous bleeding, while patients with a level above 1.0 g/L did not experience bleeds

nodes.description: Defining laboratory feature: proportionally reduced functional and antigenic plasma fibrinogen, conventionally below 1.5 g/L.

evidence.snippet: hypofibrinogenemia is characterized by fibrinogen levels lower than 1.5 g/L

nodes.description: Low plasma fibrinogen limits fibrin polymerization, so clot-based coagulation assays are prolonged in proportion to the residual fibrinogen level.

evidence.snippet: In hypofibrinogenemia, standard clotting assays are variably prolonged according to the circulating fibrinogen level.

nodes.description: Depending on the fibrinogen level, affected women may experience heavy menstrual bleeding.

evidence.snippet: hypofibrinogenemic women are also affected by menorrhagia

nodes.description: Hypofibrinogenemic women are at risk of obstetrical complications including recurrent placental abruption and pregnancy loss, reflecting the requirement of fibrinogen for placental integrity and fetal-maternal vascular development throughout pregnancy.

evidence.snippet: such as recurrent placenta abruption, suggesting insufficient fibrinogen to sustain placenta development

nodes.description: In symptomatic patients, bleeding is typically linked to trauma or surgery rather than occurring spontaneously.

evidence.snippet: the bleeding phenotype is usually linked to trauma, surgery

nodes.description: Heterozygosity for fibrinogen null or hypomorphic alleles (large deletions, frameshift, nonsense, splice-site, or secretion-impairing missense mutations) reduces the amount of normal fibrinogen produced and secreted by hepatocytes, producing proportionally low circulating fibrinogen.

evidence.snippet: Hypofibrinogenemia is generally caused by heterozygosity for these mutations.
causative mutations can be divided into two main classes: null mutations with no protein production at all and mutations producing abnormal protein chains which are retained inside the cell
